entries.title: NFL Draft: Bears pick Sun Devils LB Keyshaun Elliott at No. 166
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> picked Arizona State linebacker Keyshaun Elliott in the fifth round Saturday.<br>The team’s fifth round pick, No. 166, came from the Panthers as part of a trade in which the Bears moved up to take Texas cornerback <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/25/after-pick-swaps-with-panthers-bears-draft-texas-cb-malik-muhammad-in-fourth-round-at-no-124-overall" target="_blank" >Malik Muhammad</a> in Round 4.</p><p>Elliott, 22, started for two years at ASU after first signing to play at New Mexico State. He had nine sacks and four passes defended in 25 games in Tempe. Last season alone, he had seven sacks.</p><p>In two seasons at New Mexico State, he had 2½ sacks and 10½ tackles for loss.</p><p>"A high-end intangible player," Bears west coast area scout Reese Hicks said. "Smart, instinctive. He wore the green dot for them at Arizona State. Worked hard. Everything you want in a player, especially in Day 3 of the draft. So, we’re really happy to have him. He’s really passionate about the game. Just elite kind of intangibles and makeup."</p><p>Elliott said he heard less from the Bears than most other teams and was surprised to hear about their interest Saturday.</p><p>The suburban Kansas City, Mo., native figures to play special teams alongside Ruben Hyppolite II. He wasn't a special teams player at ASU.</p><p>"It's something I'm super-excited about," he said. "My play at linebacker is something that translates well to special teams."</p><p>The Bears are set at starting linebacker, with Devin Bush, T.J. Edwards and Jack Sanborn set to play on base downs. The 6-foot-1, 231-pounder could contribute at middle or weak-side linebacker in case of an injury.</p><p>Elliott is the fifth of 10 children.</p><p>The Bears have two more picks left in Saturday’s draft, both in Round 7.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Sky still ironing out details in preseason loss to Mercury
entries.summary: <p>SIOUX FALLS, S.D. — Coach <b>Tyler Marsh</b> wanted ball movement and player movement to be the emphasis in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky’s</a> first preseason game, and in the first quarter, it looked OK.</p><p>Their most consistent action <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/25/dawn-staley-talks-kamilla-cardoso-all-star-potential-giving-her-the-keys-to-the-car" >was finding center<b> Kamilla Cardoso</b></a> off slips, but much of their scoring in the quarter came in isolation, particularly from <b>Rickea Jackson.</b></p><p>In the second quarter of Saturday's 108-104 loss to the Mercury, the movement improved, and the Sky found<b> Rachel Banham</b> for three open 3-pointers.</p><p>“At times we did get stagnant,” Banham said after the game. “We’re still figuring out how we’re going to move together and move the ball.”</p><p>One thing the game foreshadowed was that 3-point volume could be an issue for the Sky offense. It’s hard to create movement if defenses don’t have to respect the 3-point line. The Sky shot only 16 3-pointers to the Mercury’s 34.</p><p>They can always count on Banham for the three-ball, but the offseason was more oriented around adding players who can get to the rim, like Jackson and Skylar Diggins, rather than volume shooters.</p><p>Having stretch big <b>Azurá Stevens</b>, who is out with a knee injury, will help, but likely not enough to fully solve the issue.</p><p>On the defensive end, Marsh hasn’t yet laid out exactly the style they want to play, and the game didn’t make it clear, either.</p><p>They gave up more than 50 points in the first half — and 19 alone to Mercury wing <b>Kahleah Copper.</b></p><p>To be fair, defense didn’t seem to be anyone’s priority Saturday. </p><h3><b>Strong showing from Hailey Van Lith</b></h3><p>Both teams sat their starters in the second half, instead giving minutes to players fighting for final roster spots. Sky combo guard <b>Hailey Van Lith</b> got plenty of reps with this group. She dropped 20 points on 8-for-8 shooting, with the mid-range looking lethal.</p><p>“My first stint out there I just wanted to get my legs under me, run up and down and feel the physicality,” Van Lith said after the game. “Then I was looking for the mid-range the second time. Watching Skylar pick them apart in the mid-range in the first half, I knew it would probably be there for me, too.”</p><p>She spent the offseason rehabbing an ankle injury that hampered her rookie year.</p><p>“I’ve worked hard at things I need to get better at to play more in control and make better reads,” Van Lith said.</p><h3><b>Why Sioux Falls?</b></h3><h3></h3><p>Mercury coach <b>Nate Tibbetts</b> grew up in South Dakota and coached in the G League for the Miami Heat’s affiliate, the Sioux Falls Skyforce. Their arena has looked to recruit more high-profile games, so it reached out to Tibbetts.</p><p>“It’s been really fun for my wife, Lindsay, and me,” Tibbetts said before the game. “We’ve bounced around so much chasing the game of basketball from team to team, city to city. To come home and have people put their arm around you and say they’re proud of you, it’s pretty cool.”</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.title: Tracking the Bears' undrafted free agent signings
entries.summary: <p>When the NFL Draft ended Saturday night, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> set about negotiating undrafted free agent contracts and sending out tryout invitations for the team’s rookie minicamp at Halas Hall in two weeks.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/24/first-rounder-dillon-needs-to-be-a-thrillin-fill-in-thieneman" target="_blank" >Bears</a> have had some recent success in that department, landing backup quarterback Tyson Bagent as an undrafted free agent three years ago. Receiver Jahdae Walker was an undrafted free agent last year who caught six passes, including a touchdown against the Packers in December. Cornerback and special teams stalwart Josh Blackwell was an undrafted free agent signed by the Eagles who the Bears claimed in 2022.</p><p>Here’s a look at the Bears’ undrafted free agent moves:</p><p>• Louisville quarterback <b>Miller Moss</b>, who once backed up Caleb Williams when the two were at USC, is signing with the Bears. Moss threw for 2,679 yards and 16 yards last year after spending his first four seasons with the Trojans.</p><p>• Tulane cornerback <b>KC Eziomume</b>, who played in 11 games last year. He previously played at Albany and Alabama A&M. </p><p>• Wyoming guard <b>Caden Barnett</b>, who was a second-team all-Mountain West guard last year.<br>Wyoming guard <b>Caden</b> <b>Barnett</b> is signing with the Bears as an undrafted free agent.</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Meet the Bears' rookies: Breaking down all 7 of their picks in the 2026 NFL Draft
entries.summary: <p>Here are all seven <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a>' draft picks:</p><h3><b>Round 1, No. 25</b></h3><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/24/dillon-thieneman-got-to-the-nfl-by-using-criticism-to-improve-thatll-serve-him-well-with-the-bears" >Dillon Thieneman</a><br>Oregon S<br>6-0, 201 pounds<br>Age: 21</p><p><b>Background:</b> Thieneman grew up outside of Indianapolis, followed his two older brothers’ path by going to Purdue as a safety, then transferred to Oregon with the hope of vaulting himself into a first-round prospect.</p><p><b>The stats:</b> No number stands out more than the 4.35 seconds Thieneman clocked in the 40-yard dash, which caught general manager Ryan Poles’ eye as has been obsessive about adding speed to the defense. He had eight interceptions, 10 tackles for loss and two forced fumbles in three collegiate seasons.</p><p><b>The skinny:</b> Thieneman instantly steps into the vacancy at safety next to Coby Bryant. None the Bears’ other candidates for that spot will be a legitimate contender.</p><h3><b>Round 2, No. 57</b></h3><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/24/bears-nfl-draft-logan-jones-57-second-round-pick-center-drew-dalman-garrett-bradbury" >Logan Jones</a><br>Iowa C<br>6-2, 299 pounds<br>Age: 24</p><p><b>Background:</b> Jones is Midwestern through and through, growing up in Iowa, staying in state for college, then landing with the Bears. He came up as a defensive tackle before moving to the offensive line his second season with the Hawkeyes.</p><p><b>The stats:</b> He played 52 games (51 starts) at a program reputed for coaching offensive linemen in a way that readily translates to the NFL. He won the Rimington Trophy as the best center in the country last season. NFL Network analyst Daniel Jeremiah ranked him the No. 75 overall prospect in this draft.</p><p><b>The skinny:</b> Poles made this pick with an eye on 2027. The plan is for veteran Garrett Bradbury to be the starter as Jones learns the position and cross-trains at guard.</p><h3><b>Round 3, No. 69</b></h3><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/24/nfl-draft-bears-trade-back-from-pick-60-69-144-titans-ryan-poles" >Sam Roush</a><br>Stanford TE<br>6-6, 267 pounds<br>Age: 22</p><p><b>Background:</b> Roush’s NFL lineage traces back to his grandfather, Phil Olsen, the No. 4 overall pick in 1970 and Olsen’s brothers Merlin and Orrin. Merlin Olsen was a Hall of Fame defensive tackle and actor.</p><p><b>The stats:</b> There is a lot of blocking and special teams on Roush’s résumé. As a receiver, he had his best season as a senior with 49 catches for 545 yards and two touchdowns.</p><p><b>The skinny:</b> He’s the new Durham Smythe. Don’t know who that is? Smythe was the Bears’ third tight end behind Colston Loveland and Cole Kmet last season and was a relatively subtle piece in coach Ben Johnson’s offense. He’s unlikely to cut into Loveland or Kmet’s action.</p><h3><b>Round 3, No. 89</b></h3><h3></h3><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/24/nfl-draft-bears-lsu-wr-zavion-thomas-walter-payton-relative-no-89-third-round-wide-receiver-ryan-poles" >Zavion Thomas</a><br>LSU WR<br>5-10, 190 pounds<br>Age: 22</p><p><b>Background:</b> Thomas, from the New Orleans area, played two seasons at Mississippi State before transferring for two at LSU. He ran the 40-yard dash in 4.28 seconds this offseason and has incredible start and stop ability.</p><p><b>The stats:</b> As the No. 2 wide receiver at LSU last season, Thomas caught 41 passes for 488 yards and scored four touchdowns, as well as rushing 19 times for 99 yards and a touchdown. He also had a season of 40 catches, 503 yards and one touchdown for Mississippi State in 2023.</p><p><b>The skinny:</b> Johnson fell in love with Thomas’ film as a receiver and rusher, and the Bears believe he can develop him into an explosive weapon. That’ll be mostly as a gadget player at first.</p><h3><b>Round 4, No. 124</b></h3><h3></h3><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/25/after-pick-swaps-with-panthers-bears-draft-texas-cb-malik-muhammad-in-fourth-round-at-no-124-overall" >Malik Muhammad</a><br>Texas CB<br>6-0, 182 pounds<br>Age: 21</p><p><b>Background:</b> Muhammad, from Dallas, earned a significant role at Texas as a true freshman. He might be a little small to match up with wide receivers on the outside, but the Bears plan for him to bulk up.</p><p><b>The stats:</b> He left school early after being second-team All-SEC last season with 30 tackles, two interceptions and 2 1/2 tackles for loss. He had three career interceptions in 41 games.</p><p><b>The skinny:</b> Even with Jaylon Johnson and Kyler Gordon in the building, teams can never get enough cornerbacks. Muhammad will battle Tyrique Stevenson and Terell Smith, both of whom are entering the last season of their contracts.</p><h3><b>Round 5, No. 166</b></h3><h3></h3><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/25/nfl-draft-bears-keyshaun-elliott-arizona-state-linebacker-166-fifth-round" >Keyshaun Elliott</a><br>Arizona State LB<br>6-1, 231 pounds<br>Age: 22</p><p><b>Background:</b> He’s the grandson of former Bengals and 49ers running back Lenvil Elliott, a Super Bowl champion. He grew up outside of Kansas City, began his college career at New Mexico State and played his last two seasons at Arizona State.</p><p><b>The stats:</b> Elliott had 98 tackles, including seven sacks last season. He totaled 11½ sacks, one interception and two fumble recoveries in 52 collegiate games.</p><p><b>The skinny:</b> The Bears envision Elliott competing for playing time at linebacker, where the Bears are relatively unproven after T.J. Edwards and Devin Bush, but he also has the qualities to play on all four special-teams units.</p><h3><b>Round 6, No. 213</b></h3><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/25/another-draft-day-trade-for-bears-who-jump-to-pick-georgia-tech-dt-jordan-van-den-berg-in-round-6" >Jordan van den Berg</a><br>Georgia Tech DT<br>6-3, 310 pounds<br>Age: 24</p><p><b>Background:</b> Van den Berg is South African, moved to the U.S. at 10 and grew up playing cricket, rugby, basketball and baseball before trying football in high school. He began as a linebacker, then moved to defensive end and eventually defensive tackle.</p><p><b>The stats:</b> He was an All-ACC defensive tackle last season with 44 tackles, including seven for loss. He ran a 4.94 in the 40-yard dash and has a 36-inch vertical leap.</p><p><b>The skinny:</b> Any pick at this point is a lottery ticket, and that’s the Bears’ hope with van den Berg. He’ll have a tough road to make the roster and could be a long-term project on the practice squad.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Another draft-day trade for Bears, who jump to pick Georgia Tech DT Jordan van den Berg in Round 6
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> finally made a move on their defensive line, trading up Saturday to get Georgia Tech defensive tackle Jordan van den Berg in the sixth round with the No. 213 overall pick. They sent both of their seventh-round picks, Nos. 239 and 241 overall, to the Bills for the selection. That would end the draft for the Bears unless they make a deal to get additional picks.</p><p>Van den Berg, 24, was born in Johannesburg and moved to the U.S. at 10. He began his college career as a walk-on at Iowa Western, then transferred to Penn State and eventually Georgia Tech. He had 44 tackles last season, including 11 for loss.</p><p>Even with the Bears' need to improve on the defensive line, it'll be a tough path for van den Berg to get a spot on the depth chart. Grady Jarrett and Gervon Dexter are the starting defensive tackles, with veteran Neville Gallimore and others behind them.</p><h3>Here is the Bears’ draft class so far:</h3><p><b>Round 1, No. 25 overall:</b> Oregon S <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/24/dillon-thieneman-got-to-the-nfl-by-using-criticism-to-improve-thatll-serve-him-well-with-the-bears" target="_blank" >Dillon Thieneman</a></p><p><b>Round 2, No. 57 overall:</b> Iowa C <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/24/bears-nfl-draft-logan-jones-57-second-round-pick-center-drew-dalman-garrett-bradbury" >Logan Jones</a></p><p><b>Round 3, No. 69 overall:</b> Stanford TE <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/24/nfl-draft-bears-trade-back-from-pick-60-69-144-titans-ryan-poles" >Sam Roush</a></p><p><b>Round 3, No. 89 overall:</b> LSU WR <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/24/nfl-draft-bears-lsu-wr-zavion-thomas-walter-payton-relative-no-89-third-round-wide-receiver-ryan-poles" >Zavion Thomas</a></p><p><b>Round 4, No. 124 overall:</b> Texas CB <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/25/after-pick-swaps-with-panthers-bears-draft-texas-cb-malik-muhammad-in-fourth-round-at-no-124-overall" >Malik Muhammad</a></p><p><b>Round 5, No. 166 overall:</b> Arizona State LB <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/25/nfl-draft-bears-keyshaun-elliott-arizona-state-linebacker-166-fifth-round" >Keyshaun Elliott</a></p><p><b>Round 6, No. 213 overall:</b> Georgia Tech DT Jordan van den Berg</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true"
